questions.question: What are the different types of JOINs in SQL?
questions.answer: SQL JOINs combine rows from two or more tables based on a related column. INNER JOIN: returns only rows where there is a match in BOTH tables. LEFT JOIN (LEFT OUTER): returns all rows from the left table, and matched rows from the right (NULLs where no match). RIGHT JOIN: opposite of LEFT JOIN. FULL OUTER JOIN: returns all rows from both tables, NULLs where no match. CROSS JOIN: every row of left multiplied by every row of right (Cartesian product).
questions.codeExample:
-- INNER JOIN — only customers who have orders
SELECT c.Name, o.Amount
FROM Customers c
INNER JOIN Orders o ON c.Id = o.CustomerId;

-- LEFT JOIN — ALL customers, NULLs for those with no orders
SELECT c.Name, o.Amount
FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.Id = o.CustomerId;

-- Anti-join — customers with NO orders
SELECT c.Name
FROM Customers c
LEFT JOIN Orders o ON c.Id = o.CustomerId
WHERE o.CustomerId IS NULL;
questions.simpleExample: Imagine two lists: 'Employees' and 'Departments'. INNER JOIN: only show employees who HAVE a department — no department, no row. LEFT JOIN: show ALL employees, and their department if they have one (NULL if they don't).

questions.question: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
questions.answer: WHERE filters rows BEFORE grouping — it applies to individual rows and cannot use aggregate functions (SUM, COUNT, AVG). HAVING filters groups AFTER the GROUP BY clause — it can use aggregate functions. Rule of thumb: WHERE filters individual rows; HAVING filters the grouped results.
questions.codeExample:
-- WHERE filters before grouping, HAVING filters after
SELECT CustomerId, COUNT(*) as OrderCount, SUM(Amount) as Total
FROM Orders
WHERE OrderDate >= '2024-01-01'    -- rows filtered here
GROUP BY CustomerId
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 5               -- groups filtered here
ORDER BY Total DESC;

-- ❌ Wrong — can't use aggregate in WHERE
-- WHERE COUNT(*) > 5 causes an error!
-- ✅ Correct — use HAVING for aggregates
questions.simpleExample: WHERE is the bouncer at the door — it decides which INDIVIDUAL PEOPLE get in before the party. HAVING is the manager at closing time — they look at the groups that formed and decide which GROUP stays based on how many people are in it.

questions.question: What are SQL Indexes and how do they improve performance?
questions.answer: An index is a data structure (B-tree by default) that allows the database engine to find rows without scanning the entire table. Without an index, a query does a 'full table scan' (reads every row). With an index on the searched column, it's like looking up a book in a library index — jump directly to the data. Indexes speed up reads but slightly slow down writes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE must also update the index).
questions.simpleExample: Imagine a phone book with 1 million entries. Without an index: you read every entry to find 'John Smith'. WITH an index on LastName: you flip to 'S', jump to 'Smith', done in seconds. Cost: the index takes up space and needs updating on every insert.

questions.question: What are aggregate functions and GROUP BY in SQL?
questions.answer: Aggregate functions perform calculations on a set of rows and return a single value per group. Common functions: COUNT(*) counts rows, SUM(col) totals a numeric column, AVG(col) calculates the average, MIN/MAX returns the smallest/largest value. GROUP BY divides result rows into groups — the aggregate is applied to each group separately.
questions.codeExample:
-- Basic aggregates
SELECT 
    COUNT(*) AS TotalOrders,
    SUM(Amount) AS Revenue,
    AVG(Amount) AS AvgOrderValue,
    MAX(Amount) AS LargestOrder
FROM Orders
WHERE OrderDate >= '2024-01-01';

-- GROUP BY — aggregates per group
SELECT 
    Region,
    COUNT(*) AS OrderCount,
    SUM(Amount) AS MonthlyRevenue
FROM Orders
GROUP BY Region
ORDER BY MonthlyRevenue DESC;
questions.simpleExample: GROUP BY is like sorting your receipts into separate envelopes by month. Once sorted, you can count the receipts per envelope (COUNT), add up the totals (SUM), or find the biggest purchase (MAX).

questions.question: How does NULL work in SQL and how should you handle it?
questions.answer: NULL in SQL represents the absence of a value (unknown). NULL is NOT equal to anything — not even NULL. SQL uses three-valued logic (TRUE, FALSE, UNKNOWN). Any comparison with NULL (=, <>, <, >) returns UNKNOWN, not TRUE or FALSE. Use IS NULL or IS NOT NULL to check for NULLs. Functions like COALESCE, ISNULL, NULLIF help handle NULLs safely.
questions.simpleExample: NULL means 'I don't know'. Is NULL = NULL? SQL says: 'I don't know if two unknowns are the same.' So WHERE Phone = NULL NEVER matches — use WHERE Phone IS NULL. COALESCE is your 'use this if that is missing' tool.

questions.question: What are Window Functions in SQL and how are they different from GROUP BY?
questions.answer: Window functions perform calculations across a set of rows related to the current row WITHOUT collapsing rows like GROUP BY does. You get the aggregate result PLUS the original row. Syntax: FUNCTION() OVER (PARTITION BY col ORDER BY col). Key window functions: ROW_NUMBER, RANK/DENSE_RANK, LAG/LEAD, FIRST_VALUE/LAST_VALUE, and running totals with SUM() OVER.
questions.codeExample:
-- ROW_NUMBER — unique rank per partition
SELECT 
    SalesRep, Region, Amount,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY Amount DESC) AS RankInRegion
FROM Sales;

-- Running total per region
SELECT 
    SalesRep, Region, Amount,
    SUM(Amount) OVER (PARTITION BY Region ORDER BY OrderDate
        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) AS RunningTotal
FROM Sales;

-- LAG — compare current row to previous row
SELECT OrderDate, TotalRevenue,
    LAG(TotalRevenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY OrderDate) AS PrevDayRevenue,
    TotalRevenue - LAG(TotalRevenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY OrderDate) AS Change
FROM DailySales;
questions.simpleExample: GROUP BY collapses all the sales rows for each region into ONE summary row. Window functions keep every individual row but ADD a new column showing the aggregate for the group. LAG is like saying 'what was last month's value?' without a self-join.

questions.question: What are Common Table Expressions (CTEs) and when should you use them?
questions.answer: A CTE (WITH clause) is a named temporary result set that you can reference in a SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement. CTEs improve readability by breaking complex queries into named, reusable steps. Recursive CTEs can traverse hierarchical data (org charts, trees).
questions.codeExample:
-- Simple CTE — improves readability
WITH ActiveCustomers AS (
    SELECT Id, Name, Email
    FROM Customers
    WHERE IsActive = 1
),
HighValueOrders AS (
    SELECT CustomerId, SUM(Amount) AS TotalSpend
    FROM Orders
    GROUP BY CustomerId
    HAVING SUM(Amount) > 1000
)
SELECT ac.Name, hvo.TotalSpend
FROM ActiveCustomers ac
INNER JOIN HighValueOrders hvo ON ac.Id = hvo.CustomerId;

-- Recursive CTE — traverse org hierarchy
WITH OrgHierarchy AS (
    SELECT Id, Name, ManagerId, 0 AS Level
    FROM Employees WHERE ManagerId IS NULL
    UNION ALL
    SELECT e.Id, e.Name, e.ManagerId, oh.Level + 1
    FROM Employees e
    INNER JOIN OrgHierarchy oh ON e.ManagerId = oh.Id
)
SELECT REPLICATE('  ', Level) + Name, Level FROM OrgHierarchy;
questions.simpleExample: A CTE is like defining a named formula in a spreadsheet before using it. Instead of copy-pasting the same complex subquery 3 times, define it once and reference the name. Recursive CTEs are for tree structures — start with the root (CEO), keep joining employees to their manager.

questions.question: How does query optimization work and what tools can you use?
questions.answer: Query optimization involves writing queries that the engine can execute efficiently. Key tools: EXPLAIN/EXPLAIN ANALYZE (PostgreSQL), SET STATISTICS IO ON + Execution Plan (SQL Server). Look for: Table Scan (bad — add index), Index Seek (good). Key practices: avoid SELECT *, use appropriate indexes, avoid functions on indexed columns in WHERE, avoid N+1 query patterns.
questions.simpleExample: SELECT * is like packing your entire house for a weekend trip — bring only what you need. A function in WHERE (YEAR(OrderDate) = 2024) is like arriving at the GPS destination then walking 2km — rewrite so the GPS (index) gets you to the exact spot. N+1 queries are 1000 trips to the grocery store for 1 item each.

questions.question: What are Transactions in SQL and what are the ACID properties?
questions.answer: A transaction is a sequence of operations treated as a single logical unit of work. Either all operations succeed (COMMIT) or all are undone (ROLLBACK). ACID properties: Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (data remains in a valid state), Isolation (concurrent transactions don't interfere), Durability (committed data persists even after crashes).
questions.simpleExample: A transaction is like an ATM transfer. You take $500 from Account A and add $500 to Account B. If the power cuts out after the debit but before the credit, you've lost $500! A transaction makes these two operations ATOMIC — either both happen or neither does.

questions.question: What are Stored Procedures vs Views vs Functions in SQL?
questions.answer: Views are named queries — a virtual table that encapsulates a SELECT. They simplify complex queries but cannot have parameters. Stored Procedures are compiled SQL code blocks that can have parameters, contain DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), and include control flow. Functions (scalar or table-valued) return a value and can be used directly in SELECT queries; they cannot have side effects.
questions.simpleExample: A View is a window with a permanently saved view (saved SELECT). A Stored Procedure is a script — it can do many things, accept inputs, insert/update data. A Function is a formula — it takes inputs and returns a value you can use inside another query.
